I just used an analogy between software development and human integrity in an argument with my sister that I think it's worth recording

So one of the most well well-known principles of the 'Clean Code' philosophy is that software tests (basically a side program that verifies if your main program is working properly) have to be written BEFORE you your main program.

This way you force yourself to actually think about the main program at a deeper level than you would otherwise. You're no longer just focused on making it work, you're trying to find keystones of what you're building.

If you wait for your main program to be working before writing the tests, you take the risk of overfitting the tests to what you've already written. You're not actually testing if the program is functional, only that it doesn't crash (which despite popular belief is not the same)

Now to the good stuff: If you as a human do not have a set of values and ideologies to live by on a grande scale you risk taking decisions that you wouldn't otherwise just because it benefits you on the short term.

You try to justify it to yourself with arguments that aren't completely true, just because you "want" the conclusion to be one that benefits you. While that is very natural do humans, it may not be the morally current and result in later repercussions that are hard to deal with

That being said, much like in software development, those values do not have to follow you for the rest of your life. Tests can lose its relevance once the main program has evolved. Once you learn more about the world and yourself you can start believing in different things.
My main point is just that one must decide what it stands for and keep it in mind when making decisions throughout life otherwise we risk becoming someone we're not really proud of.

The dynamic activity of data science and the static nature of code are quite difficult to balance.

Data science is all about: How can I create a function out of this data that allows me to make accurate predictions for the future.

Its success depends on the algorithm you choose to model the function AND how you choose to treat and feed that data to the algorithm.
(Both of these steps are highly simplified here, there are infinite rabbit holes of theory in both of them)

To get this right, you need tons and tons and tons of exploration. Not only on both algortithms, methodology, hyper-parameters, the list goes on. For most of the time you. Are constantly changing your workflow as experiments go on.

Code is static though. It was meant to always expect the same kind of stimulus, process them and to respond accordingly. 

Data science requires a flexibility that code really doesn't offer inherently.
And I know what coders think while reading this: 'Well, whether it works or not, really depends on how you write the code'. Abstraction and pre-planning are key.

I absolutely agree! And it must be possible to build beautiful abstracted data science pipelines, I'm just taking so much time to find where the key lies. 

I just feel like it is so much easier in other domains like distributed systems or web dev.

I've been working in DS/ML for longer that I worked in web now, and while I feel like my intuitions and capabilities have defenitly increased I'm still never super-proud of the code I have by the end of a project.

Then again maybe it's not really fair to compare it to web since I got to work with a really amazing team that gave me great insights right off the bat, while on ML I'm kind of always figuring it out on my own or with friends that are on the same level as me.

Yesterday me and @manichaeistic came across what is known as a Heisenbug - a software bug that seems to disappear or alter its behavior when one attempts to study it, named after the Heisenberg principle. 

These exist, people, the machine is not always right!

For those curious, the bug is not fully fixed yet, but we've been able to pinpoint it to a really specific part of the code. 

What causes the inconsistency are operations on realllyyyy small floats (10^-15) which apparently can be non deterministic.

After the February/March markets breakdown, I decided to invest part of my savings. This was something that I wanted to do for a while, and the crash made it seem like the perfect time.

At the time there was a tone of uncertainty, and no one knew what was going to happen. It could have gotten much worse before it got better. 

As a caution, I spaced out my investments across three consecutive months. I invested in both ETFs and crypto.

The idea for ETFs was (and still is) that I'm in it for the long run. As much as I know, I'll only withdraw this money to retire on. I used an app called etfmatic (Robo advisor), set up my profile, decided on my exposure to risk and that was that.

The algorithms spread out my investments across the global economy in both stock ETFs (80%) and bond ETFs (20%).

~ 10 months later: 26% gains.

For crypto, however, I got in honestly... just because it showed great upside. It's a new exciting field that no one can really predict. The amount I started with was much smaller when compared to ETFs (~12%). 

80% of it went to Bitcoin, while the remaining 20% went for Ethereum
The main difference between the 2 investments, and which I'm struggling with now, is that I never really had a plan for crypto - just got in because historically, it seemed like a good deal.
I was prepared to lose any money that went into it. However, ~ 10 months later, I'm up around ~500% and I'm not really sure what the move is here.

I don't really have any merit in these gains other than the sense of opportunity and the commitment to actually do it. Other than that it was mostly luck.
I didn't know that I was entering at basically the bottom of one hell of a bullish run.

I know it's kind of ironic to say, but I'm not even sure how much I believe in crypto. The future of money is definitely digital, but what's going to be mainstream (in my opinion) are the digital versions of the currency that already exist (digital euro, digital RMB...)

Most people don't really care that central banks control it, actually, they feel a sense of security - even though they probably shouldn't sometimes. 

Stable coins, backed by tangible assets, provide that security.
What happens to BTC in that scenario? Might still be useful for a lot of people, and it might still be extremely valuable but will it live up to the hype?

Some enthusiasts even believe that the entire global economy could run on BTC. 

Personally, I think that'd be a disaster, the Matthew effect would eventually break society and we'd evolve into a pure Capitalist dystopia.

Todo o processo de "corridas eleitorais" é inútil e serve mais para entretenimento do que para dar a conhecer os candidatos. 

1/7

Só existe uma coisa que interessa quando escolher em quem votar/apoiar: dados históricos de voto.

Hoje em dia pouco interessa que doutrina um partilho diz defender - a doutrina é algo teórico e em muitas vezes de pouca utilidade prática.

2/7

Principalmente num sistema em que os partidos estão constantemente a sacrificar ideologia por influência.

Não interessa a doutrina, não interessam discursos, nao interessam cartazes, não interessa posts em redes sociais. 

3/7

Só uma coisa interessa - como é que as pessoas agiram em situações do passado e como é que se alinha com aquilo que eu defendo? Tudo o resto é barulho. 

Uma plataforma de visualizações de dados que permite a análise desta métrica em detalhe poderia revolucionar a política.

4/7

Com a ferramenta certa, que permitisse uma investigação sobre o historial de todos os partidos - qualquer pessoa percebia rapidamente, com poucos minutos ou horas de investigação, qual o candidato que melhor o representanta. Baseado somente em dados históricos de ação

5/7

Uma ferramenta deste tipo conseguia não só retirar o poder a pessoas que dependem exclusivamente de retórica e discursos bonitos para ganhar apoio, como também incutia pressão para votos mais responsáveis na assembleia.

6/7

Marcar os nossos ideias com etiquetas de esquerda, direita, e tudo mais serve, apenas para nos limitar e dividir.

7/7

Programming projects easily reach complexity levels where you feel that your brain does not process the necessary dimensions to keep up with.

This is mostly because of :

1) growing code bases without enough (and proper) abstraction layers.

2) The medium of information representation through which we code which is solely with text.

Text is awesome - it's increasingly explicit and allows a mixture of representing functionality via our own semantics.

However, it's incredible that there are clearly a lack of visual tools to help out with taming the beasts that some code bases become.

I'm sure there are plenty of cool stuff out there and I'm searching for them as we speak but I'm pretty sure that it is not standard to use them - which it should. 

Collaboration becomes so much harder when everything you have to go on are endless piles of text.

@woonomic They're important for different aspects. The 1st for every day life, the second for savings. But if I had to choose one, the reliable unit of acount has to take precedent - although I don't think that for today's way of life that's as easy of a decision as it seems.

I just heard about Conway's Law:

Any organization that designs a system (defined broadly) will produce a system whose structure is a reflection of the organization's communication structure

Startups and even larger companies end up creating fragmented products due to siloed teams. 

Conway's Law tells us the reason: poor communication between teams leads to disjointed user experience.

Thinking about how traditional tech team splits (frontend vs. backend) can be limiting. 

Data modals and endpoint structure end up not being optimal for FE logic.

Full-stack teams, organized by feature sets might more easily lead to unified software solutions?

This law is a cool asset. By intentionally designing our organization, we design our products. 

Emphasizing open, cross-functional communication leads to more cohesive and integrated solutions.

Basically building cool tech is also building teams and culture.

This law suggests these aspects are intertwined which I think it's a very cool learning to materialize and grasp.
@sreinsights Definitely! It's not just a theory but a practical observation.

SLO platforms restrict users to a single compliance window (cw) for analysis.

From an operational perspective, i understand: terms like 'minutes left in our monthly budget' become benchmarks for discussion. But can this single lens capture the full picture?

The cw is one of the building blocks that the original literature define for SLOs (along with the SLI, the target value, the evaluation criteria).

However, a fixed compliance window restricts our ability to explore the rich landscape of SLOs in different temporal contexts.

Visualizing SLOs along different windows unlocks new dimensions for reporting and assessing degradation of performance. 
It also allows power-users to dig in deeper, while the rest of the organization continues to use the default cw for discussion, alignment, OKRs and EB policies
